questions.scenario:
The subnet named `orders-public-a` has this route table:

```text
Destination      Target
10.20.0.0/16     local
```

No internet gateway route is associated with it. What should the team conclude?

options.text: The subnet is public because its name and intended role establish exposure even when the route table has only the local route.
The name says public, but the route table does not prove public internet-bound routing.
A resource with a public IPv4 address would make this subnet public without adding an internet-gateway route.
Adding a NAT gateway default route would make the subnet a public entry point for unsolicited internet traffic.

questions.explanation:
Public and private behavior comes from routing. Without a route to an internet gateway, the subnet name alone does not make internet-bound traffic public. Names help humans, but route tables decide behavior.

Learn more in [VPC route tables](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/VPC_Route_Tables.html).

questions.scenario:
The app runs in private subnets and logs:

Request is POST https://api.taxvendor.example/v1/quote. Cause is ConnectTimeoutError: connect ETIMEDOUT 203.0.113.20:443.

The VPC has endpoints for S3, ECR, Secrets Manager, and CloudWatch Logs. What is still likely missing?

options.text: An S3 gateway endpoint, because all HTTPS traffic uses S3 behind the scenes.
A public IP on RDS, because tax providers call back to the database.
A controlled outbound path for true internet destinations, such as NAT or an approved proxy.
A Route 53 alias from the vendor domain to the orders ALB.

questions.explanation:
VPC endpoints help with supported AWS services. A tax provider endpoint is a true external destination, so the private task needs a controlled egress path. Keep NAT or proxy access focused on real external needs.

Learn more in [NAT gateways](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-nat-gateway.html).

questions.scenario:
A DevPolaris operator cannot reach a production EC2 instance from the approved administration address. The instance has a public address, so the operator compares its subnet and security controls.

```text
Instance public IPv4: 198.51.100.44
Subnet route table:
10.40.0.0/16 -> local
Security group inbound:
TCP 22 from 203.0.113.10/32
```

An operator cannot SSH from the approved IP. What should the team notice?

options.text: Broaden the SSH security-group source to the office CIDR before fixing the subnet's missing internet-gateway route.
Add a Systems Manager role but leave the instance without the endpoint or outbound management path it needs.
The instance has a public address, but the subnet route table has no internet gateway route.
Move SSH behind a bastion but leave both the bastion and target subnet without a route that supports the intended connection.

questions.explanation:
A public address alone does not prove the public path works. The subnet also needs the right internet gateway route, and the security group must allow the caller. The intended goal decides whether to add the public route or remove the public address.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario: The DevPolaris security team discovers that `devpolaris-orders-prod` became publicly accessible during troubleshooting. Its subnet group now uses `orders-public-a` and `orders-public-b`, and its security group allows TCP 5432 from `0.0.0.0/0`. The database must return to private data subnets and accept connections only from the orders application tier. Which changes belong in the fix plan?

options.text: Disable public accessibility for the database.
Keep the public subnet group but rely on a longer database password as the network control.
Move the database into a private DB subnet group.
Replace broad inbound TCP 5432 with the app task security group as the source.
A private subnet with a NAT route also accepts unsolicited inbound connections through that NAT gateway.

questions.explanation:
The snapshot has several public exposure signals at once. The safer shape is private placement, no public database address, and a narrow source from the app tier. A strong password is necessary, but it is not a substitute for network exposure control.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris network team is reviewing where to place the public entry point, application tasks, and database for devpolaris-orders-api shows:

Orders-alb is internet-facing. Orders-api tasks is should receive traffic only from ALB. Orders-db is should receive traffic only from app tasks.

Which subnet plan best matches that intent?

options.text: ALB, ECS tasks, and RDS all in public subnets so each layer can be tested from a browser.
ALB in public subnets, ECS tasks in private app subnets, RDS in private database subnets.
ALB in private subnets and ECS tasks in public subnets so the app can receive internet traffic first.
RDS in public subnets and ALB in database subnets so the database owns the public entry point.

questions.explanation:
The public edge belongs in public subnets, while the app and database stay private behind intended callers. That separates the browser entry point from runtime and data storage. It is a clean first production shape for this service.

Learn more in [VPC subnet basics](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/configure-subnets.html).

questions.scenario: A private ECS task stops before application startup. IAM simulation confirms that its task execution role can retrieve the referenced secret, but the task event reports `ResourceInitializationError: unable to retrieve secret` and a connection timeout to `secretsmanager.us-east-1.amazonaws.com`. The subnet has only the local VPC route and no Secrets Manager interface endpoint. What should the network team verify next?

options.text: Whether Route 53 points `orders.devpolaris.com` at the production ALB and the public name resolves from the task subnet.
Whether the database security group allows TCP 5432 from the ALB security group and the RDS endpoint resolves privately.
Whether the ALB health check accepts HTTP 500 during startup and its grace period covers secret retrieval.
Whether the private subnet can reach Secrets Manager through a NAT path or an interface VPC endpoint whose security group permits HTTPS from the tasks.

questions.explanation:
The IAM check passed, while the ECS agent timed out reaching the specific Secrets Manager endpoint before the application started. The next investigation is therefore the private outbound path, endpoint security group, and private DNS behavior for Secrets Manager. Route 53 public entry, database ingress, and ALB health checks occur on different paths.

Learn more in [Using Secrets Manager with VPC endpoints](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/secretsmanager/latest/userguide/vpc-endpoint-overview.html).

questions.scenario:
The task keeps restarting with:

ResourceInitializationError failed to pull image: dial tcp 54.239.31.88:443: i/o timeout

```text
Private app route table:
10.20.0.0/16 -> local
```

No ECR endpoints are configured. Which fix direction matches the failure?

options.text: Add the needed outbound path for image pull, such as NAT or the ECR-related VPC endpoints.
Open ALB inbound TCP 443 from the task security group.
Point the Route 53 alias directly at the ECS service.
Move RDS into the public ALB subnets.

questions.explanation:
This is private task outbound access during initialization. The task has no default route and no ECR endpoint path, so it cannot reach the registry. Public DNS and database placement are not the first broken direction.

Learn more in [VPC endpoint concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/privatelink/concepts.html).
